ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea class (FOODON_03301103); no specific Dong Ding cultivar entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers oolong tea as a category; no Dong Ding-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance; no Dong Ding-specific concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed Dong Ding Oolong Tea is approximately 240 ml, equivalent to a typical single-serve beverage portion as served in Taiwanese tea houses and Singaporean bubble tea establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 173228)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 policy) entry for brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 173228) is used as the closest available and nutritionally equivalent match. Dong Ding is a specific cultivar of oolong tea; when brewed plain, its macronutrient and micronutrient profile is considered nutritionally identical to generic brewed oolong tea. This adaptation is fully compliant with Veritas v2 source policy, as no specific entry for Dong Ding Oolong Tea exists in higher-priority regional databases like HPB Singapore Foodo. Values represent plain brewed tea without added milk or sugar, consistent with the food's primary identity.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Plain brewed tea (unsweetened); analogous estimate for zero-carbohydrate beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database (an approved GI-specific source per Project Veritas v2 policy) is the authoritative source for glycemic data. Plain brewed oolong tea contains negligible carbohydrates (approximately 0.2 g per 100 g) and no glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies unsweetened teas and similar zero-carbohydrate beverages as having no measurable glycemic response. In accordance with the Project Veritas v2 'Zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule', the glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value, representing effectively zero GI), gi_category is set to 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because the carbohydrate content per serving is below the threshold for a measurable glycemic load. This application of the rule is a direct implementation of Veritas policy for such beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content; polyphenol content may support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed Dong Ding Oolong Tea contains approximately 3 mg sodium per 100 ml and is free of added salt, making it a suitable beverage for individuals managing hypertension; however, sweetened or milk-added commercial preparations should be assessed separately for sodium and calorie content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and zero glycemic load when consumed plain
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed Dong Ding Oolong Tea has a glycemic load of effectively zero and contains no added sugars, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; individuals should avoid sweetened or condensed-milk versions, which substantially increase the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat; tea polyphenols and catechins may confer cardioprotective effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed Dong Ding Oolong Tea contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and no trans fat, and the polyphenolic compounds in oolong tea have been associated in observational studies with modest improvements in LDL oxidation and endothelial function, supporting its inclusion in a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dong Ding Oolong Tea (冻顶乌龙茶 / 凍頂烏龍茶) is a premium Taiwanese oolong tea originating from the Dong Ding mountain area of Nantou County, Taiwan. The name translates literally as 'Frozen Summit' or 'Icy Peak', referring to the high-altitude tea gardens where the leaves are cultivated. It is produced from the Camellia sinensis plant and undergoes partial oxidation (typically 15–40%), placing it between green tea and black tea on the oxidation spectrum. A defining characteristic of Dong Ding Oolong is its traditional charcoal-roasting process, which imparts a distinctive deep, toasty aroma with caramel and honey undertones, setting it apart from lighter, floral oolongs such as High Mountain (Alishan) oolong. The brewed liquor is typically golden-amber in colour with a smooth, full-bodied mouthfeel, a lingering sweet aftertaste, and low astringency. Nutritionally, plain brewed Dong Ding Oolong Tea is essentially a zero-calorie, zero-fat, zero-protein beverage with negligible carbohydrate content (approximately 0.2 g per 100 ml) and very low sodium (approximately 3 mg per 100 ml). It contains naturally occurring polyphenols including catechins, theaflavins, and thearubigins, as well as caffeine (approximately 30–50 mg per 240 ml serving). These compounds have been associated in observational research with antioxidant activity, modest improvements in lipid profiles, and potential benefits for glycaemic regulation. The glycemic index is effectively zero and the glycemic load per serving is 0.0, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or milk. In Singapore, Dong Ding Oolong Tea is served at Taiwanese-style tea houses and specialty bubble tea chains such as CHICHA San Chen, where it may be served hot or iced, plain or with added toppings. Consumers should note that commercial preparations with added sugar, condensed milk, or toppings will substantially alter the nutritional profile.

Is Dong Ding Oolong Tea vegan and halal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for generic brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 173228), which is an approved secondary global source and the closest available match. No Singapore-specific or Dong Ding-specific entry exists in higher-priority regional databases like HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. The adaptation is nutritionally defensible because the brewed beverage profile of Dong Ding Oolong is not materially different from generic brewed oolong tea when consumed plain. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact cultivar and roasting process are not captured in the generic USDA entry.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g (naturally trace); this reflects the USDA SR Legacy value for brewed oolong tea. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed plain tea contains no protein detectable above reporting thresholds. fat_g is naturally zero as brewed plain tea contains no fat. sugar_g is naturally zero for plain unsweetened brewed tea. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the tea leaves are not consumed. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages, per Project Veritas v2 zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is below the threshold for a measurable glycemic load. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central for brewed oolong tea; confirmed zero. 4. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0 as reported by USDA FoodData Central for brewed oolong tea; confirmed zero or below detection threshold (trace). 5. The field survey listing includes 'Milk Tea' as a cuisine tag, but the food record describes the tea itself (Dong Ding Oolong Tea) without milk; nutrient values reflect plain brewed tea. Milk-added or sweetened variants would require a separate record. 6. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no Dong Ding-specific codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 7. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this food is served in Singapore, despite its Taiwanese origin.
